stem: Decompose into partial fractions with distinct linear factors and evaluate \( \displaystyle\int_{[[a]]+[[p]]}^{[[a]]+[[p]]+[[q]]} \frac{1}{(x-[[a]])(x-[[b]])}\,dx \).
solution: Write \( \dfrac{1}{(x-[[a]])(x-[[b]])} = \dfrac{1}{[[a]]-[[b]]}\left(\dfrac{1}{x-[[a]]} - \dfrac{1}{x-[[b]]}\right) \). Integrating gives \( \dfrac{1}{[[a]]-[[b]]}\ln\left|\dfrac{x-[[a]]}{x-[[b]]}\right| \), and evaluating over the given bounds yields \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mismatches which root's shift goes in the numerator versus the denominator of the log ratio.
This flips the sign of the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Decompose into partial fractions with a repeated linear factor and evaluate \( \displaystyle\int_{[[b]]+[[m]]}^{[[b]]+[[m]]+2} \frac{x}{(x-[[a]])(x-[[b]])^{2}}\,dx \).
solution: Write \( \dfrac{x}{(x-[[a]])(x-[[b]])^{2}} = \dfrac{A}{x-[[a]]} + \dfrac{B}{x-[[b]]} + \dfrac{C}{(x-[[b]])^{2}} \), with \( A = \dfrac{[[a]]}{([[a]]-[[b]])^{2}} \) and \( C = \dfrac{[[b]]}{[[b]]-[[a]]} \) found by clearing denominators, and \( B \) from matching the remaining coefficient. Integrating term by term (the \( C \) term gives a \( -1/(x-[[b]]) \) antiderivative, not a log) and evaluating over the bounds gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes the repeated-factor terms cancel out over these bounds.
This flips the sign of the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Decompose into partial fractions with an irreducible quadratic factor and evaluate \( \displaystyle\int_{1}^{[[m]]+1} \frac{[[b]]}{x\left(x^{2}+[[a]]\right)}\,dx \).
solution: Write \( \dfrac{[[b]]}{x(x^{2}+[[a]])} = \dfrac{[[b]]/[[a]]}{x} - \dfrac{([[b]]/[[a]])x}{x^{2}+[[a]]} \). The first piece integrates to a log of \( x \), and the quadratic piece integrates to a log of \( x^{2}+[[a]] \) (an irreducible quadratic contributes a logarithm here, not an arctangent, because its numerator is proportional to \( x \)). Evaluating from \( 1 \) to \( [[m]]+1 \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This keeps only the \( 1/x \) piece of the decomposition and drops the quadratic-factor term entirely.
This flips the sign of the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Suppose \( \dfrac{[[c]]x+[[d]]}{(x-[[a]])(x-[[b]])} = \dfrac{A}{x-[[a]]} + \dfrac{B}{x-[[b]]} \) for all \( x \) where both sides are defined. What is \( A \)?
solution: Multiply both sides by \( (x-[[a]])(x-[[b]]) \) and set \( x=[[a]] \) so the \( B \) term vanishes: \( A = \dfrac{[[c]]\cdot[[a]]+[[d]]}{[[a]]-[[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This computes \( B \) (evaluating the numerator at \( x=[[b]] \)) instead of \( A \).
This uses the correct numerator value at \( x=[[a]] \) but the wrong sign in the denominator.
This adds \( [[a]]+[[b]] \) in the denominator instead of subtracting.
This only reports the numerator's linear coefficient, ignoring the residue evaluation entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The degree of the numerator here EQUALS the degree of the denominator, so first do polynomial division before decomposing. Evaluate \( \displaystyle\int_{[[b]]+[[m]]+1}^{[[b]]+[[m]]+3} \frac{x^{2}}{(x-[[a]])(x-[[b]])}\,dx \).
solution: Since the numerator and denominator have the same degree, \( \dfrac{x^{2}}{(x-[[a]])(x-[[b]])} = 1 + \dfrac{([[a]]+[[b]])x - [[a]]\cdot[[b]]}{(x-[[a]])(x-[[b]])} \) after division, and the remaining proper fraction decomposes into two linear terms. Integrating the constant \( 1 \) plus the two logarithms and evaluating over the bounds gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports only the constant term \( 1 \) integrated over an interval of length 2, ignoring the logarithmic partial-fraction remainder entirely.
This flips the sign of the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
